output.summary_final: {"title": "Building Trails That Last: Skills, Bike Maintenance, and Smart Riding", "bullets": ["Speed comes from controlled braking and smooth inputs rather than taking bigger risks on the trail.", "On berms, focus on eyes through the exit, hips centered, and pressure building gradually to maintain flow and control.", "Well-draining trails preserve soil structure and reduce rutting, keeping them rideable longer and safer.", "A short, consistent bike maintenance routine—checking bolts, chain wear, stanchions, and pads—keeps the bike quiet and predictable.", "Tire pressure and casing choice often have a bigger impact on performance than minor suspension adjustments.", "For climbs, maintain a consistent cadence to reduce drivetrain stress and improve traction management.", "In wet conditions, prioritize decision-making and knowing when to back off to protect traction and trails.", "Improvement happens faster by repeating one trail segment and changing only one variable at a time during practice.", "Mystery creaks are usually fixed by cleaning contact surfaces, re-greasing threads, and re-torquing to spec.", "Start simple, repeat the habit, and log observations to iterate calmly and improve over time."]}

{"title": "Designing sustainable on-call and secure systems", "bullets": ["A healthy on-call rotation minimizes wake-ups and makes responses obvious, relying on clear ownership and well-defined procedures.", "Short-lived, narrowly scoped secrets with automated rotation improve security and reduce exposure risks.", "Threat modeling can be lightweight: identify assets, entry points, trust boundaries, and likely abuse cases to guide security decisions.", "Writing down assumptions and tradeoffs in RFCs turns disagreement into collaborative problem-solving and explicit risk assessment.", "Error budgets should drive behavior change, not just sit as unread charts during planning meetings.", "Alerting must be action-oriented; if no one can act immediately, the alert shouldn’t page.", "Healthy systems expect failures and contain them with timeouts, retries, and clear ownership to reduce blast radius.", "Security works best when the secure path is the easiest path, avoiding reliance on shame or heroics.", "Start small and repeat: document observations to iterate calmly and improve systems over time."]}

{"title": "Wreck Diving Fundamentals: Calm, Planning, and Redundancy", "bullets": ["Calm breathing and deliberate decisions are the foundation of safe diving, especially in challenging environments like wrecks.", "Use a primary and backup navigation reference—such as heading plus depth or reef contour plus current direction—to maintain orientation underwater.", "Practicing neutral buoyancy drills like hovering and slow fin pivots reduces task load and improves control in choppy conditions.", "A backup light isn’t just redundancy; it shifts mindset by providing options and reducing stress during dives.", "The ability to call a dive early without ego is the most valuable skill to prevent unnecessary risks and surprises.", "Dive plans should be simple, shared, and include clear turn pressures and exit routes to minimize confusion underwater.", "Most underwater stress stems from surprise combined with lack of rehearsal, not the inherent difficulty of the problem.", "Regulator free-flows are alarming but manageable when practiced calmly; preparation reduces panic in emergencies."]}

{"title": "Underwater Photography: Lighting, Routines, and Simplicity", "bullets": ["Great underwater images start with a clear idea and a simple, consistent setup rather than complex gear.", "Small changes in strobe angle reduce backscatter by moving light away from the lens axis, improving clarity underwater.", "Use grids to control light spill and keep unwanted areas dark, focusing attention on the subject.", "In mixed lighting, choose a dominant color temperature and correct other sources to match for cohesive results.", "Label files and write captions the same day to prevent memory gaps that can derail long-term projects.", "Documentary work thrives on routines like backups, notes, and small habits to avoid catastrophic data loss.", "Practice with one light to master placement and placement’s impact on shadows and viewer focus.", "Underwater photography relies more on lighting and positioning than on the camera itself as a limiting factor.", "Start simple, repeat the process, and document observations to iterate calmly and improve consistently."]}

{"title": "Index Investing Without the Myths: Costs, Behavior, and Risk", "bullets": ["Risk is not volatility; it’s the chance of failing to meet goals or suffering permanent loss, so focus on outcomes rather than short-term price swings.", "Index investing succeeds by cutting costs and reducing decision errors, not by predicting markets or picking winners.", "Position sizing rules protect portfolios from a single great idea turning into a catastrophic loss.", "Fees quietly erode returns over time, so lowering costs is like earning extra returns without added risk.", "Behavior is the hardest part—staying disciplined when markets are down and headlines are loud requires pre-defined rules and stress testing.", "A simple stress test asks if you can handle higher rates, longer vacancies, and unexpected repairs without panic selling.", "Diversification must mean different drivers, not just different tickers that move together in lockstep.", "Drawdowns are both mathematical and emotional; a 50% loss requires a 100% gain to recover, which can derail long-term plans.", "Start small, repeat consistently, and journal observations to refine your approach without emotional reactions."]}
